meta.title: Frontend Engineer Interview Study Plan
meta.description: Comprehensive interview topics for a Senior Frontend Engineer specialising in Angular

weeks.label: Week 1–2
weeks.title: Angular Deep Dive
weeks.focus: Consolidate your strongest area — be able to articulate the internals clearly, not just use them

topics.title: Signals & Zoneless Change Detection
topics.summary: Signal graph, effect(), computed(), and why Zoneless is a performance win.
interviewQuestions: What problem do Signals solve that Zone.js didn't?
What's the difference between set(), update(), and mutate()?
Can you use Signals without Zoneless?
What happens if you forget to call a signal in a template — {{ count }} instead of {{ count() }}?
talkingPoints: Built Pulse in Angular 21 fully Zoneless from day one
Used toSignal() to bridge RxJS HTTP calls into the signal graph
Zero unnecessary re-renders — verifiable in DevTools

concepts.name: Signal graph
concepts.detail: Signals track who reads them and notify dependents when they change. Only affected components re-render.

concepts.name: Reactive contexts
concepts.detail: Signals must be read inside a reactive context — template, computed(), or effect() — to be tracked.

concepts.name: toSignal / toObservable
concepts.detail: Bridge between RxJS Observables and the Signals world. Used for async data from HTTP calls.

concepts.name: Zoneless bootstrapping
concepts.detail: provideZonelessChangeDetection() removes Zone.js entirely. Requires OnPush or Signals for all updates.

topics.title: Angular 14–17 Migration Patterns
topics.summary: Standalone components, new control flow syntax (@if/@for/@switch), and deferrable views (@defer).
interviewQuestions: What's the main benefit of standalone components over NgModules?
Why is track mandatory in @for?
How does @defer differ from route-level lazy loading?
Can you use NgModules and standalone components in the same app?
talkingPoints: Migrated incrementally at ParkBee — leaf components first
Used @defer for below-the-fold content alongside route-based splitting
Circular import bugs surfaced and resolved during standalone migration

concepts.name: Standalone components
concepts.detail: Components that declare their own dependencies directly. No NgModule required. Enables tree-shaking per component.

concepts.name: Control flow syntax
concepts.detail: @if, @for, @switch replace *ngIf, *ngFor, *ngSwitch. No CommonModule import needed. Better type narrowing.

concepts.name: @defer blocks
concepts.detail: Declarative component-level lazy loading. Triggers: on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on timer, when condition.

concepts.name: Migration strategy
concepts.detail: Leaf components first → feature modules → bootstrap migration → delete AppModule. ng generate @angular/core:standalone automates it.

topics.title: SSR — Hydration, TransferState, SEO
topics.summary: How Angular Universal works, hydration mechanics, avoiding double data fetching, and common pitfalls.
interviewQuestions: What is hydration and why does it matter?
What causes hydration mismatches and how do you fix them?
What's the difference between SSR and prerendering?
How do you avoid fetching data twice — once on the server and once on the client?
talkingPoints: ParkBee SSR strategy for SEO and bounce rate improvement
Audited codebase for window/localStorage usage — wrapped with isPlatformBrowser
Implemented withHttpTransferCache to stop double data fetching

concepts.name: Hydration
concepts.detail: Angular reattaches event listeners to server-rendered HTML without destroying the DOM. Enabled via provideClientHydration().

concepts.name: Hydration mismatches
concepts.detail: Server and client render different HTML. Causes: window/localStorage access, Math.random(), Date.now(). Fix: isPlatformBrowser() guards.

concepts.name: TransferState
concepts.detail: Passes server-fetched data to the client inside the HTML, preventing double API calls. withHttpTransferCache() automates HTTP calls.

concepts.name: SSR vs SSG vs prerendering
concepts.detail: SSR: per-request. SSG: build-time. Prerendering: build-time for specified routes. Use SSR for personalised/dynamic data.

topics.title: Change Detection Internals
topics.summary: OnPush vs Default vs Zoneless, markForCheck vs detectChanges, detach/reattach, and reading flame graphs for CD issues.
interviewQuestions: What's the difference between Default and OnPush change detection?
When would you use markForCheck() vs detectChanges()?
How does OnPush interact with Signals?
What causes ExpressionChangedAfterItHasBeenCheckedError?
How would you debug a performance problem caused by change detection?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: scroll listeners triggering CD cycles — fixed with runOutsideAngular()
Eliminating long tasks via CDP profiling — recurring ApplicationRef.tick() calls
Pulse: Zoneless from day one — no unnecessary CD cycles by design

concepts.name: Default vs OnPush
concepts.detail: Default checks every component on every CD cycle. OnPush skips unless: input reference changes, event fires, async pipe emits, or manual trigger.

concepts.name: markForCheck() vs detectChanges()
concepts.detail: markForCheck(): schedules check next cycle, marks ancestors dirty. detectChanges(): synchronous and immediate, checks component and children.

concepts.name: runOutsideAngular()
concepts.detail: Runs code without triggering Zone.js. Use for high-frequency events (scroll, mousemove). Use ngZone.run() when UI actually needs updating.

concepts.name: Flame graph for CD
concepts.detail: Look for recurring ApplicationRef.tick() calls. checkAndUpdateView repeated = deep tree being fully checked. Zone catching high-frequency events.

topics.title: DI System & Hierarchical Injectors
topics.summary: EnvironmentInjector vs ElementInjector, providedIn options, inject() function, InjectionToken, and provider types.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between ElementInjector and EnvironmentInjector?
When would you scope a service to a component rather than root?
What's the difference between @Self(), @SkipSelf(), and @Host()?
Why is providedIn: 'root' preferred over NgModule providers arrays?
Where can you call inject() and where can't you?
talkingPoints: Cuurios: WebSocket service scoped to dashboard component — destroyed with component, no memory leak
Refactored root-scoped socket to component-scoped — fixed persistent open connection bug

concepts.name: Injector tree
concepts.detail: NullInjector → EnvironmentInjector (root) → EnvironmentInjector (lazy) → ElementInjector chain. Resolution walks upward.

concepts.name: providedIn options
concepts.detail: 'root': app-wide singleton, tree-shakeable. 'platform': across multiple Angular apps. Component providers: scoped to component subtree.

concepts.name: inject() function
concepts.detail: Modern alternative to constructor injection. Works in field initialisers and injection contexts. Enables composable injection logic.

concepts.name: InjectionToken
concepts.detail: For non-class dependencies: config objects, strings, functions. Typed with generics. Supports factory functions for default values.

concepts.name: Provider types
concepts.detail: useClass: swap implementations. useValue: static values. useFactory: dynamic/conditional. useExisting: alias one token to another.

weeks.label: Week 2–3
weeks.title: Performance Engineering
weeks.focus: Your strongest differentiator — package the Lighthouse 33→75 story and close the INP and memory leak gaps

topics.title: Core Web Vitals
topics.summary: LCP, CLS, and INP — thresholds, causes, measurement, and Angular-specific fixes.
interviewQuestions: What are the three Core Web Vitals and their good thresholds?
What replaced FID and why?
What causes CLS and how do you prevent it?
How does NgOptimizedImage help LCP?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: CloudFront CDN reduced TTFB → improved LCP
Route-based code splitting reduced TTI
SSR improved FCP for first-time visitors

concepts.name: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint)
concepts.detail: Good: < 2.5s. Measures time until largest visible element renders. Fix: CDN, optimised images, fetchpriority='high', preconnect, SSR.

concepts.name: CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift)
concepts.detail: Good: < 0.1. Measures unexpected layout movement. Fix: explicit image dimensions, aspect-ratio CSS, font-display: optional, reserved space for dynamic content.

concepts.name: INP (Interaction to Next Paint)
concepts.detail: Good: < 200ms. Replaced FID in March 2024. Measures worst interaction delay across entire session. Fix: task chunking, scheduler.yield(), debouncing.

topics.title: Code Splitting & Lazy Loading
topics.summary: Route-based, component-level (@defer), and dynamic import strategies. Preloading strategies.
interviewQuestions: What are the three levels of code splitting in Angular?
How does @defer differ from loadComponent()?
When would you use a custom preloading strategy?
How do you lazy-load a third-party library?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: route-based code splitting was core to Lighthouse 33→75 improvement
@defer used for below-the-fold content — component-level splitting without TypeScript changes

concepts.name: Route-based splitting
concepts.detail: loadComponent() for standalone components, loadChildren() for route arrays. Separate bundle per route, downloaded on navigation.

concepts.name: Component-level splitting
concepts.detail: @defer blocks — declarative lazy loading triggered by viewport, idle, interaction, timer, or condition.

concepts.name: Dynamic import
concepts.detail: import('library') defers library loading until needed. Useful for heavy libs (xlsx, chart.js) only used on specific interactions.

concepts.name: Preloading strategies
concepts.detail: PreloadAllModules: load all lazy chunks after initial load. Custom strategy: preload only routes marked with data.preload = true.

topics.title: CDP & Flame Graph Analysis
topics.summary: Chrome DevTools Protocol profiling workflow, reading flame graphs, identifying CD issues and forced reflows.
interviewQuestions: Walk me through how you'd profile a slow Angular app.
What does a CD performance problem look like in a flame graph?
What is a forced reflow and how do you avoid it?
How did you improve Lighthouse from 33 to 75?
talkingPoints: CDP recording revealed two problems: Zone.js catching scroll events + no code splitting
Flame graph showed recurring ApplicationRef.tick() from map integration scroll listeners
Batch DOM reads before writes to avoid forced reflow

concepts.name: Performance recording workflow
concepts.detail: CPU throttle 4x, Network Fast 3G → Record → identify long tasks (red) → drill into flame graph → find self time → fix → re-measure.

concepts.name: CD in flame graph
concepts.detail: zone.js onInvokeTask → ApplicationRef.tick → checkAndUpdateView (repeated) = unnecessary full CD cycles. Move listeners outside zone.

concepts.name: Forced reflow
concepts.detail: Reading layout properties (offsetHeight) after writing styles forces synchronous layout recalculation. Batch reads before writes.

topics.title: INP Optimisation Patterns
topics.summary: Scheduler API, task chunking, requestAnimationFrame vs setTimeout, web workers, and debouncing heavy handlers.
interviewQuestions: What is INP and how does it differ from FID?
How do you fix a heavy click handler that blocks paint?
What is the Scheduler API and when would you use it?
How would you move heavy computation off the main thread?
talkingPoints: Angular-specific: debounce filter input with RxJS, move filtering to web worker
runOutsideAngular() for high-frequency events connects directly to INP improvement

concepts.name: INP measurement
concepts.detail: Input delay + processing time + presentation delay for the worst interaction in a session. Threshold: < 200ms good, > 500ms poor.

concepts.name: scheduler.yield()
concepts.detail: Pauses execution and yields to the browser, allowing paint and input handling. Resume after with await. Chunks long synchronous work.

concepts.name: scheduler.postTask()
concepts.detail: Priority-based task scheduling: user-blocking, user-visible, background. Schedule non-urgent work as background to keep UI responsive.

concepts.name: Task chunking
concepts.detail: Break large array processing into batches with setTimeout(fn, 0) between chunks. Browser can paint and handle input between chunks.

topics.title: Memory Leak Detection
topics.summary: The three common Angular leak types, takeUntilDestroyed, DestroyRef, heap snapshots in DevTools.
interviewQuestions: What are the most common memory leaks in Angular?
How do you detect a memory leak using DevTools?
What is takeUntilDestroyed and how does it work?
When would you use WeakRef?
talkingPoints: Cuurios: WebSocket service leaked until scoped to component — destroyed on navigation
ParkBee: event listeners on window needed explicit removal in ngOnDestroy

concepts.name: Unsubscribed observables
concepts.detail: Subscriptions that outlive their component. Fix: takeUntilDestroyed(destroyRef) in Angular 16+, or async pipe which auto-unsubscribes.

concepts.name: Leaked event listeners
concepts.detail: window/document listeners added in ngOnInit must be removed. Use DestroyRef.onDestroy() for clean registration/removal in one place.

concepts.name: Detached DOM nodes
concepts.detail: Component destroyed but a reference prevents GC. Common in services holding component arrays. Use WeakRef to allow GC.

concepts.name: Heap snapshot workflow
concepts.detail: Baseline snapshot → navigate to component → interact → navigate away → force GC → new snapshot → compare → look for Detached nodes.

weeks.label: Week 3–4
weeks.title: Architecture & System Design
weeks.focus: Turn your Nx monorepo and micro-frontend experience into a structured design vocabulary

topics.title: Micro-Frontend Architecture
topics.summary: Module Federation, composition strategies, shared state patterns, routing in MFE, and Nx monorepo approach.
interviewQuestions: When do micro-frontends make sense and when don't they?
How do you handle shared state across micro-frontend boundaries?
How do you manage Angular version conflicts in Module Federation?
How does an Nx monorepo differ from full micro-frontend deployment independence?
talkingPoints: Cuurios: Nx monorepo with shared action schema across three micro-applications
Shared library versioning enforced via nx enforce-module-boundaries

concepts.name: Module Federation
concepts.detail: Webpack 5 feature. Shell (host) loads remote bundles at runtime. Shared dependencies (Angular core) declared to avoid loading twice.

concepts.name: Composition strategies
concepts.detail: Build-time (npm packages), runtime (Module Federation), server-side (ESI fragments). Choose based on team autonomy and deployment needs.

concepts.name: Cross-MFE state
concepts.detail: Prefer: CustomEvents on window (loose coupling), URL as shared truth, shared npm package for critical state. Avoid: shared NgRx store across boundaries.

concepts.name: Nx monorepo
concepts.detail: MFE benefits (shared libs, module boundaries) without full deployment independence. nx affected only rebuilds/tests what changed.

topics.title: State Management Trade-offs
topics.summary: Decision framework for signals vs NgRx, NgRx Signal Store, when classic NgRx is justified, and the comparison table.
interviewQuestions: How would you decide between Signals and NgRx for a new feature?
What is NgRx Signal Store and how does it differ from classic NgRx?
When is the boilerplate of classic NgRx actually worth it?
How do you handle state that needs to be shared across a feature but not app-wide?
talkingPoints: Cuurios: NgRx for real-time KPI dashboard — complex async flows, multiple derived views
Pulse: Signals throughout — simpler app, no need for action audit trail

concepts.name: Decision framework
concepts.detail: Local UI state → signals. Feature state shared across components → service with signals + scoped provider. Complex async + devtools → NgRx Signal Store. Audit log needed → classic NgRx.

concepts.name: NgRx Signal Store
concepts.detail: Modern NgRx (v17+). signalStore() with withState(), withComputed(), withMethods(). Much less boilerplate than classic NgRx. Has devtools support.

concepts.name: Classic NgRx
concepts.detail: Actions as explicit events (audit log). Reducers as pure functions. Effects for async. Justified for large teams needing time-travel debugging or compliance audit trails.

concepts.name: Facade pattern
concepts.detail: Injectable service that hides NgRx/signals behind a stable API. Components never import from @ngrx/store directly. Enables state implementation swaps.

topics.title: Frontend System Design Interviews
topics.summary: The 40-minute framework, real-time dashboard design, autocomplete design, and edge case thinking.
interviewQuestions: Design a real-time KPI dashboard.
Design a search autocomplete component.
Design an infinite scroll list.
Design a notification system.
talkingPoints: Cuurios real-time KPI dashboard: WebSocket + RxJS + NgRx, 25% reduction in analyst effort
Always lead with clarifying questions before jumping to implementation

concepts.name: 40-minute framework
concepts.detail: 0–5: clarify. 5–10: data model. 10–25: core architecture. 25–35: edge cases (errors, loading, offline). 35–40: performance and scale.

concepts.name: Real-time dashboard patterns
concepts.detail: WebSocket + exponential backoff reconnection. Batch UI updates (throttle). runOutsideAngular() for socket messages. OnPush everywhere.

concepts.name: Autocomplete patterns
concepts.detail: debounceTime(300) + distinctUntilChanged + switchMap (cancel in-flight). Min char threshold. Keyboard nav (arrow/enter/escape). ARIA combobox pattern.

concepts.name: Edge cases to always mention
concepts.detail: Empty state, error state, loading skeleton, network offline, race conditions, keyboard accessibility, mobile responsiveness.

topics.title: Design Patterns for Scale
topics.summary: Container/presentational, facade, command pattern, and when each applies in Angular.
interviewQuestions: What is the container/presentational pattern and why does it matter?
When would you use the facade pattern?
How does the command pattern apply to configurable UI workflows?
What design patterns have you used in production Angular apps?
talkingPoints: Cuurios Workflow Actions system: command pattern for configurable operational flows
ParkBee: container/presentational separation made ANPR dashboard components individually testable

concepts.name: Container/Presentational
concepts.detail: Container: owns data fetching and state, connected to services/store. Presentational: OnPush, purely reactive to inputs, emits events via outputs. Independently testable.

concepts.name: Facade pattern
concepts.detail: Injectable service hiding NgRx/signals complexity. Components get a clean API. Implementation can change without touching components.

concepts.name: Command pattern
concepts.detail: Actions as data (JSON). Executor maps action types to handler classes. New action types added by registering handlers — open/closed principle.

concepts.name: Smart/dumb components
concepts.detail: Alternative naming for container/presentational. Dumb components should be reusable across contexts. Smart components should be thin orchestrators.

topics.title: API Design from a Frontend Perspective
topics.summary: REST vs GraphQL trade-offs, pagination patterns (offset vs cursor), optimistic updates, and error handling.
interviewQuestions: When would you recommend GraphQL over REST?
What is cursor pagination and why is it better than offset for real-time data?
How do optimistic updates work and what's the rollback strategy?
How do you handle global error handling in Angular?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: GraphQL with Strapi CMS for marketing/SEO content — schema integrations in monorepo
Snapp! fintech: API design constraints informed by Iranian banking rail requirements

concepts.name: REST vs GraphQL
concepts.detail: REST: HTTP caching, CDN-friendly, simple. GraphQL: client-specified fields, no over/under-fetching, built-in schema, complex caching. Choose REST unless data complexity justifies GraphQL.

concepts.name: Cursor pagination
concepts.detail: Stable cursor position regardless of inserts/deletes. No page drift. Response includes nextCursor and hasMore. Required for real-time or frequently updated data.

concepts.name: Optimistic updates
concepts.detail: Update UI immediately, confirm with server async. On error: roll back to previous state and show error. Improves perceived performance for common actions.

concepts.name: Global error handling
concepts.detail: Implement ErrorHandler. Log to Datadog/Sentry. Show user-friendly message. Never expose stack traces in production. Re-throw in development mode.

weeks.label: Week 4–5
weeks.title: React & Breadth
weeks.focus: Understand React's mental model deeply enough to reason about it and frame your level honestly

topics.title: React Hooks Mental Model
topics.summary: useState, useEffect, useCallback, useMemo, useRef, useReducer, and custom hooks. The stale closure problem.
interviewQuestions: What is the fundamental difference between Angular components and React function components?
What is the stale closure problem and how do you fix it?
When should you use useCallback and when is it overkill?
What is the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect?
talkingPoints: React function re-runs entirely on every render — closures capture values at render time
Angular class instance persists — this.count always current. React closure can be stale.
Custom hooks = Angular's injectable services with inject() pattern

concepts.name: Component re-render model
concepts.detail: React components are functions — called on every render. Hooks 'opt out' for specific values. Angular has persistent class instances. This difference explains stale closures.

concepts.name: Stale closure
concepts.detail: useEffect with [] captures values at mount time. Counter inside setInterval will always see initial value. Fix: include in deps array, or use useRef to hold current value.

concepts.name: useMemo vs useCallback
concepts.detail: useMemo: memoises a value (like computed()). useCallback: memoises a function reference. Use only when expensive or when referential equality matters for child re-renders.

concepts.name: Custom hooks
concepts.detail: Extract reusable stateful logic. Equivalent to Angular's injectable services + inject() pattern. Convention: name starts with 'use'. Can use other hooks inside.

topics.title: React State Management
topics.summary: useState vs useReducer vs Context vs Zustand vs React Query. When each applies.
interviewQuestions: When would you use useReducer instead of useState?
What's the performance problem with overusing Context?
What is React Query and how does it differ from useState for server data?
How does Zustand compare to NgRx Signal Store?
talkingPoints: Zustand ≈ Injectable service with signals. No Provider needed. Minimal boilerplate.
React Query ≈ Angular's HTTP + async pipe + caching. Handles loading/error/stale states automatically.
Context performance: split by update frequency — auth context separate from theme context

concepts.name: useState vs useReducer
concepts.detail: useState for simple independent values. useReducer when multiple related values change together based on action types — mirrors NgRx reducer pattern.

concepts.name: Context limitations
concepts.detail: Every consumer re-renders when any context value changes. Split contexts by update frequency. Not suitable for high-frequency state (use Zustand or local state instead).

concepts.name: Zustand
concepts.detail: Lightweight global state. create() returns a hook. No Provider. No boilerplate. Set state directly. Angular equivalent: Injectable service with signals.

concepts.name: React Query
concepts.detail: Server state management: fetching, caching, background refresh, optimistic updates. useQuery for reads, useMutation for writes. Replaces manual loading/error useState patterns.

topics.title: Concurrent Features & Suspense
topics.summary: React 18 concurrent rendering, useTransition, Suspense boundaries, Error Boundaries.
interviewQuestions: What does concurrent rendering mean in React 18?
When would you use useTransition?
How does Suspense compare to Angular's @defer?
Why are Error Boundaries still class components?
talkingPoints: Suspense ≈ Angular @defer — both provide declarative loading states for async content
useTransition ≈ scheduler.postTask() with user-visible priority — non-urgent updates can be interrupted

concepts.name: Concurrent rendering
concepts.detail: Rendering is interruptible in React 18. Browser can pause a render to handle urgent input, then resume. Enables prioritised updates without janky UI.

concepts.name: useTransition
concepts.detail: Mark state updates as non-urgent. Urgent updates (typing) are not blocked by non-urgent renders (filtering results). isPending flag shows when transition is in progress.

concepts.name: Suspense
concepts.detail: Declarative loading states. Component 'suspends' (throws a Promise), Suspense shows fallback until resolved. Nest Suspense boundaries for granular loading UI.

concepts.name: Error Boundaries
concepts.detail: Class components that catch render errors in their subtree. getDerivedStateFromError() shows fallback. componentDidCatch() logs the error. No hook equivalent yet.

topics.title: Next.js Fundamentals
topics.summary: App Router, server vs client components, data fetching patterns (static/dynamic/revalidate), parallel fetching.
interviewQuestions: What is the difference between Server and Client components?
What does 'use client' actually do?
How does Next.js App Router handle caching for fetch calls?
How do you avoid waterfall data fetching in Next.js?
talkingPoints: Server components = Angular SSR with direct DB access, zero client bundle impact
Client components = Angular standalone components with browser APIs and hooks

concepts.name: Server components (default)
concepts.detail: Run only on server. Can access DB directly. Zero JS sent to client. Cannot use hooks or browser APIs. Best for data fetching and rendering.

concepts.name: Client components ('use client')
concepts.detail: Opt-in with 'use client' directive. Can use hooks and browser APIs. Add interactivity at the leaves of the component tree. Minimise their use.

concepts.name: Fetch caching options
concepts.detail: force-cache: static (like SSG). no-store: dynamic (like SSR per request). next.revalidate: ISR (cached, refresh every N seconds).

concepts.name: Parallel data fetching
concepts.detail: Promise.all([fetchA(), fetchB()]) fires both simultaneously. Sequential awaits create waterfall. Always parallelise independent data dependencies.

topics.title: Framing Your React Level Honestly
topics.summary: The Angular→React translation table, React.memo vs OnPush, and the one-sentence positioning statement.
interviewQuestions: How would you describe your React experience level?
How does React.memo relate to Angular's OnPush?
What would be your ramp-up plan joining a React codebase?
What are the biggest mental model shifts from Angular to React?
talkingPoints: Positioning: 'Primary depth is Angular. I understand React's hooks model and can ramp quickly because I think in components, reactivity, and state regardless of syntax.'
React.memo + stable useCallback references ≈ OnPush + immutable inputs

concepts.name: Angular → React translation
concepts.detail: signal() ↔ useState. computed() ↔ useMemo. effect() ↔ useEffect. inject(Service) ↔ custom hook. ngOnDestroy ↔ useEffect cleanup. OnPush ↔ React.memo.

concepts.name: Key mental model shift
concepts.detail: Angular: class persists, change detection is external. React: function re-runs, closures capture state at render time. Stale closures are the most common trap.

concepts.name: React.memo
concepts.detail: Prevents re-render if props haven't changed by reference. Requires stable prop references — use useCallback for functions passed as props to memo'd children.

weeks.label: Week 5
weeks.title: Testing & Accessibility
weeks.focus: Deepen the 'why' behind your testing decisions and be ready to implement WCAG specifics live

topics.title: Testing Philosophy
topics.summary: Testing trophy vs pyramid, behaviour vs implementation testing, decision framework for what to test.
interviewQuestions: What is the testing trophy and how does it influence your strategy?
What's the difference between testing behaviour and testing implementation?
What should you always test vs never test?
How do you balance test coverage with delivery speed?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: owned Jest unit coverage and reviewed all Playwright E2E specs
Testing trophy: integration tests give best ROI — test how components work together

concepts.name: Testing trophy
concepts.detail: Static (TypeScript/ESLint) → Unit (fast, isolated) → Integration (best ROI) → E2E (few, slow, high confidence). Integration tests are the investment focus.

concepts.name: Behaviour vs implementation
concepts.detail: Test what users experience, not internal method calls. Tests that survive refactoring are testing the right thing. Avoid spying on private methods.

concepts.name: What to always test
concepts.detail: Business logic, conditional rendering, user interactions, error states, accessibility. Never test: framework internals, third-party libraries, simple passthroughs.

topics.title: Jest Patterns for Angular
topics.summary: TestBed, ComponentFixture, signals in tests, HttpTestingController, spy vs stub vs mock, snapshot testing.
interviewQuestions: How do you test a component that uses HttpClient?
What's the difference between a spy, a stub, and a mock?
How do you test signal-based components?
When are snapshot tests useful and when are they harmful?
talkingPoints: Signals are synchronous — no async needed for state changes in tests
HttpTestingController: intercept → flush → verify pattern

concepts.name: TestBed setup
concepts.detail: configureTestingModule() with imports (standalone components) and providers (mocked services). fixture.detectChanges() triggers ngOnInit and initial render.

concepts.name: Spy vs stub vs mock
concepts.detail: Spy: wraps real implementation, observe calls. Stub: replaces with controlled return value. Mock: complete replacement of dependency with jest.fn() object.

concepts.name: HttpTestingController
concepts.detail: Intercepts HTTP calls in tests. Pattern: trigger call → expectOne(url) → flush(mockData) → verify(). Use afterEach to verify no unexpected requests.

concepts.name: Testing signals
concepts.detail: Signals are synchronous — set signal values directly, call fixture.detectChanges(), assert DOM state. No fakeAsync needed for pure signal state changes.

topics.title: WCAG 2.1 AA Applied
topics.summary: POUR principles, keyboard navigation implementation, ARIA attributes, focus management in modals, skip links, colour contrast.
interviewQuestions: What ARIA attributes does a custom dropdown need?
How do you implement focus trapping in a modal?
What is the difference between aria-label and aria-labelledby?
What is :focus-visible and why use it instead of :focus?
talkingPoints: ParkBee: ANPR operator dashboard keyboard navigation — operators need to act in under 3 seconds
WCAG AA contrast ratio: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text and UI components

concepts.name: POUR principles
concepts.detail: Perceivable (alt text, contrast), Operable (keyboard nav, no seizure content), Understandable (clear language, predictable), Robust (valid HTML, ARIA, screen reader tested).

concepts.name: Keyboard navigation
concepts.detail: Tab: focus. Enter/Space: activate. Arrow keys: navigate within widgets. Escape: close/cancel. Home/End: jump to first/last. Every interaction must be keyboard-reachable.

concepts.name: Focus management
concepts.detail: On modal open: save previous focus, move focus into modal. On modal close: return focus to trigger. Trap Tab/Shift+Tab within modal boundary.

concepts.name: aria-live regions
concepts.detail: role='alert' / aria-live='assertive': immediate announcement (errors). aria-live='polite': waits for user idle (status updates). Use for dynamic content injected after page load.

concepts.name: :focus-visible
concepts.detail: Shows focus ring only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks. Replace outline: none with :focus-visible custom style. Minimum 3:1 contrast ratio for focus indicator.

topics.title: Testing Async Code
topics.summary: fakeAsync/tick, flush(), testing Observables with done(), jest fake timers, testing NgRx effects.
interviewQuestions: What's the difference between fakeAsync/tick and done() in Angular tests?
How do you test a component with debounceTime?
How do you test that switchMap cancels previous requests?
How do you test NgRx effects?
talkingPoints: fakeAsync + tick(300) = precise debounce testing without waiting for real time
jest.useFakeTimers() for non-Angular async — retry logic, exponential backoff

concepts.name: fakeAsync and tick()
concepts.detail: Takes control of time in Angular tests. tick(ms) advances virtual time. flush() completes all pending timers. Deterministic and fast — no real waiting.

concepts.name: Testing debounced observables
concepts.detail: Wrap test in fakeAsync(). Trigger input multiple times. tick(300) to advance past debounce. Assert search fired once with last value.

concepts.name: Jest fake timers
concepts.detail: jest.useFakeTimers() in beforeEach, jest.useRealTimers() in afterEach. jest.advanceTimersByTime(ms) controls setTimeout/setInterval.

concepts.name: Testing NgRx effects
concepts.detail: provideMockActions() injects a ReplaySubject as actions$. Dispatch action → subscribe to effect$ → assert dispatched action matches expected.

weeks.label: Week 6
weeks.title: Interview Readiness
weeks.focus: Consolidation — story prep, career break narrative, JS fundamentals, mock interviews, and polished questions

topics.title: STAR Stories
topics.summary: Five core stories fully structured: Lighthouse 33→75, ANPR delivery, User Migration, Cuurios Workflow, Snapp! fintech.
interviewQuestions: Tell me about yourself.
Describe your biggest technical challenge.
Tell me about a time you took full ownership.
Describe a system you designed.
Tell me about working at scale.
talkingPoints: Story 1: Lighthouse 33→75 — CDP profiling → runOutsideAngular + code splitting + CloudFront
Story 2: ANPR integration — sole FE, on-site testing, WebSocket with backoff
Story 3: User Migration — state machine + AI tooling, 30% mobile adoption, ahead of schedule
Story 4: Workflow Actions — command pattern, Nx shared schema, non-technical user empowerment
Story 5: Snapp! fintech — 50M users, A/B testing, Best PM 2020, Direct Debit industry first

concepts.name: STAR framework
concepts.detail: Situation (10%), Task (10%), Action (60%), Result (20%). Target: 90 seconds. Interviewers care about what you did and how you thought — not the backstory.

concepts.name: Story mapping
concepts.detail: Performance challenge → Story 1. Ownership/delivery → Story 2. AI tooling/deadline → Story 3. System design → Story 4. Scale/leadership → Story 5.

concepts.name: Common mistakes
concepts.detail: Over-investing in Situation, under-investing in Action. Using 'we' when you should say 'I'. Not quantifying the result. Going over 90 seconds.

topics.title: Career Break Narrative
topics.summary: One-sentence delivery, follow-up responses, what not to say, and how to pivot to Pulse.
interviewQuestions: Can you explain the gap on your CV?
Are you fully ready to commit to a new role?
Were you doing anything technical during your break?
talkingPoints: One sentence: 'I took a career break from October 2025 to April 2026 for a personal health matter — fully resolved. I stayed active technically by building Pulse, an Angular 21 app with Zoneless change detection and live AI API integration.'
Deliver at normal pace. Then stop. Don't add more.
Pulse turns the gap into a positive signal — cutting-edge Angular 21 patterns

concepts.name: Core principle
concepts.detail: State it clearly, point to Pulse, move forward. The break is only as big a deal as you make it. Confidence signals there's nothing to worry about.

concepts.name: What not to do
concepts.detail: Don't apologise. Don't over-explain the health situation. Don't say 'just taking time for myself' (too vague). Don't let it become the centre of the conversation.

concepts.name: The Pulse pivot
concepts.detail: Angular 21, Zoneless, Signals, live AI API integration — these are genuinely cutting-edge. You may be more current on modern Angular than candidates in production codebases.

topics.title: JS/TS Fundamentals
topics.summary: Event loop, closures, prototypal inheritance, TypeScript utility types, discriminated unions, and common senior JS questions.
interviewQuestions: Explain the event loop — what order does this code execute?
What is a closure? Give an example of a bug it can cause.
What is prototypal inheritance?
What are TypeScript utility types? Name five.
What is the difference between null and undefined?
talkingPoints: Microtasks (Promises) always drain before macrotasks (setTimeout)
Stale closure in React useEffect = same mechanism as classic var loop bug
Discriminated unions + switch = exhaustive type checking at compile time

concepts.name: Event loop order
concepts.detail: Synchronous code → microtask queue (Promise.then, queueMicrotask) → macrotask (setTimeout, setInterval). Microtasks drain completely before next macrotask.

concepts.name: Closures
concepts.detail: Function retains access to lexical scope after outer function returns. Bug: var in for loop — all callbacks share same i. Fix: let creates new binding per iteration.

concepts.name: TypeScript utility types
concepts.detail: Partial<T>, Required<T>, Readonly<T>, Pick<T,K>, Omit<T,K>, Record<K,V>, NonNullable<T>, ReturnType<T>, Parameters<T>. Know how to implement them with mapped types.

concepts.name: Discriminated unions
concepts.detail: Union type with a common literal field (status). Switch on the discriminant — TypeScript narrows the type in each branch. Exhaustive checking with never.

topics.title: Live Coding Practice
topics.summary: The meta-skill of narrating while coding, five practice exercises, and handling gaps out loud.
interviewQuestions: Build a debounced search component in Angular.
Implement infinite scroll.
Write a retry with exponential backoff using RxJS.
Flatten a nested array without Array.flat().
Implement a deep clone function.
talkingPoints: Narrate the entire time — interviewers assess thinking process, not just output
If stuck: 'I'm not sure of the exact API here — but the approach would be...'
State assumptions out loud before coding

concepts.name: The meta-skill
concepts.detail: Silence is deadly in live coding. Say what you're doing and why. If stuck, describe the approach. Interviewers can help if they know where you are.

concepts.name: Exercise 1: Debounced search
concepts.detail: FormControl + valueChanges + debounceTime(300) + distinctUntilChanged + filter(len >= 2) + switchMap + takeUntilDestroyed. Signal for results and loading state.

concepts.name: Exercise 2: Infinite scroll
concepts.detail: IntersectionObserver on sentinel element. Load next page when sentinel enters viewport. Signal for items[], loading, hasMore. Disconnect observer on destroy.

concepts.name: Exercise 3: Retry with backoff
concepts.detail: RxJS retry({ count: 3, delay: (err, n) => timer(1000 * 2^(n-1)) }). Or retryWhen + scan + delayWhen. Note: in production use the built-in retry operator.

topics.title: Questions to Ask Interviewers
topics.summary: Fifteen questions across five categories: technical health, team structure, growth, the interviewer personally, and the role.
talkingPoints: Technical health: 'What does your deployment pipeline look like — how long from commit to production?'
Team structure: 'How are technical decisions made — tech lead, consensus, RFC?'
Growth: 'What's the hardest frontend problem the team is dealing with right now?'
Personal: 'What made you join, and what's kept you here?'
Role: 'What's the biggest gap you're hoping this hire fills?'

concepts.name: Why questions matter
concepts.detail: The questions you ask reveal seniority. Junior: salary/benefits. Mid-level: tech stack. Senior: constraints, decisions, and problems. Two or three questions maximum.

concepts.name: Reading the room
concepts.detail: Early startup → ownership and tech debt. Scale-up → structure and design system. Enterprise → autonomy and legacy. Technical interviewer → hardest problem question.

concepts.name: Questions to always have ready
concepts.detail: 'What would a strong first 90 days look like?' and 'What's the biggest gap this hire fills?' — these work in every context and signal business maturity.

topics.title: The Full Interview Loop
topics.summary: What to expect at each stage, the CV walkthrough structure, and how to handle questions you don't know.
talkingPoints: CV walkthrough: identity (20s) → strongest experience anchor (60s) → career break (15s) → intent (15s)
5 stages: recruiter screen → hiring manager → technical → senior engineer deep dive → final values round
When stuck: describe the approach, don't go silent

concepts.name: CV walkthrough structure
concepts.detail: Don't narrate chronologically. Lead with identity → anchor to strongest work → career break one sentence → close with intent. Total: ~110 seconds.

concepts.name: Handling unknown questions
concepts.detail: Don't know at all: reason from related knowledge. Partially know: be explicit about what you know and don't. Know but forgot syntax: describe the approach, say you'd check docs.

concepts.name: Stage goals
concepts.detail: Recruiter: logistics. Hiring manager: make them want to go deeper. Technical: show thinking process. Deep dive: demonstrate senior depth. Final: values alignment.
